variables.v: Speed of light in the medium (m/s)

locked_facts: Huygens' principle: every point on a wavefront acts as a source of secondary spherical wavelets
The new wavefront at a later time is the forward envelope (tangent surface) of all secondary wavelets
Backward wavelets are ignored — Huygens postulated this; later justified by Kirchhoff's diffraction theory
Derivation of Snell's law from Huygens' principle: sinθ_i/sinθ_r = v₁/v₂ = n₂/n₁
Huygens' principle predicts that light travels SLOWER in a denser medium (v₂ < v₁ when n₂ > n₁) — confirmed experimentally by Foucault
Huygens' principle explains diffraction: wavelets from edges of an aperture spread into the shadow region
truth_statements: Huygens' principle is a geometrical construction that explains how wavefronts propagate — each point on a wavefront is treated as a new point source of secondary wavelets
The new wavefront after time Δt is the forward tangent surface to all secondary wavelets of radius vΔt
Using Huygens' principle, Snell's law of refraction can be derived by considering wavefronts entering a medium where the wave speed changes
Huygens' construction correctly predicts that light slows down in a denser medium, unlike Newton's corpuscular theory which predicted the opposite
Huygens' principle naturally explains diffraction — wavelets from the unobstructed part of the wavefront spread around obstacles and into the geometric shadow

aha_moment.what_to_show: A plane wavefront approaches the boundary between two media (n₁ < n₂). In the denser medium, secondary wavelets travel slower, so their radii are smaller. The new wavefront (tangent to smaller circles) tilts toward the normal — this IS refraction. No ray theory needed, just wavelet geometry. Simultaneously show Newton's prediction (faster in denser → bends away from normal) which is WRONG.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Side-by-side: Huygens' construction giving correct refraction vs Newton's corpuscular prediction giving wrong bending direction. Wavelets in the denser medium are visibly smaller (slower speed).
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that refraction is not caused by particles speeding up in glass (Newton's wrong model) but by waves slowing down — Huygens' geometrical proof is visually compelling

simulation_states.label: Wavefront and secondary sources
simulation_states.description: A plane wavefront is shown at time t. Several equally spaced points on the wavefront are marked as secondary sources. Each source is labelled as a 'Huygens source'. The wavefront is moving to the right.
simulation_states.emphasis: Every point on the wavefront is a secondary source — this is the core of Huygens' principle

simulation_states.label: Secondary wavelets expand
simulation_states.description: After a short time Δt, each secondary source has produced a spherical wavelet of radius vΔt. The wavelets are drawn as small circles expanding from each source point. The forward envelope of these circles forms the new wavefront.
simulation_states.emphasis: Each wavelet has radius vΔt — the new wavefront is the common tangent to all forward wavelets

simulation_states.label: Refraction derived from Huygens' principle
simulation_states.description: The wavefront hits a boundary between two media (v₁ > v₂). In medium 2, wavelets travel slower so their radii are smaller. The tangent to the smaller wavelets makes a different angle — the wavefront bends toward the normal. sinθ_i/sinθ_r = v₁/v₂ = n₂/n₁ is displayed.
simulation_states.emphasis: Slower speed in denser medium → smaller wavelets → wavefront bends toward normal. This is the wave explanation of Snell's law.

simulation_states.label: Interactive exploration
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts the refractive indices of both media and observes how the wavefront bending changes. A 'diffraction mode' shows wavelets spreading past an obstacle edge into the shadow region. A 'reflection mode' shows Huygens' construction for the law of reflection.
simulation_states.emphasis: Try different n₂/n₁ ratios. Switch to diffraction mode to see wavelets spreading around obstacles. Verify the law of reflection geometrically.

common_misconceptions.belief: Light speeds up when it enters a denser medium like glass, which is why it bends
trigger_phrases: denser medium mein speed badhti hai
common_misconceptions.correction: Light SLOWS DOWN in a denser medium (v = c/n, so higher n means lower speed). Huygens' principle correctly predicts this: smaller wavelets in the denser medium cause the wavefront to bend toward the normal. Newton's corpuscular theory wrongly predicted light speeds up in glass — this was disproved by Foucault's experiment in 1850.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show wavelets in glass being smaller (slower speed) than in air, with the wavefront clearly bending toward the normal due to the speed reduction

common_misconceptions.belief: Huygens' principle only works for refraction, not for other wave phenomena
common_misconceptions.correction: Huygens' principle is a general wave construction that explains reflection, refraction, AND diffraction. For diffraction, wavelets from the unobstructed part of the wavefront spread around obstacles into the geometric shadow — no additional principle is needed.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show three scenarios using the same Huygens construction: reflection from a mirror, refraction at a boundary, and diffraction around an obstacle edge

legend.meaning: Speed of light in the medium = c/n (m/s)
Radius of secondary wavelet after time Δt
